[Lemont](/wiki/Lemont_Township,_Cook_County,_Illinois "Lemont Township, Cook County, Illinois"), [Downers Grove](/wiki/Downers_Grove_Township,_Illinois "Downers Grove Township, Illinois"), [DuPage](/wiki/DuPage_Township,_Will_County,_Illinois "DuPage Township, Will County, Illinois"), [Homer](/wiki/Homer_Township,_Will_County,_Illinois "Homer Township, Will County, Illinois")

is a village located in [Cook](/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois "Cook County, Illinois"), [DuPage](/wiki/DuPage_County,_Illinois "DuPage County, Illinois"), and [Will](/wiki/Will_County,_Illinois "Will County, Illinois") counties in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), and is a south-west suburb of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). The population was 17,629 as of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The village is situated on a hillside along the south banks of the [Des Plaines River](/wiki/Des_Plaines_River "Des Plaines River"). It overlooks [Waterfall Glen's](/wiki/Waterfall_Glen_Forest_Preserve "Waterfall Glen Forest Preserve") [Midwestern Bluff Savanna](/wiki/Oak_savanna "Oak savanna") on the opposite side. Lemont is home to [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory") and other heavy industrial sites, and has a substantial European immigrant population.

**Lemont** is a village located in [Cook](/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois "Cook County, Illinois"), [DuPage](/wiki/DuPage_County,_Illinois "DuPage County, Illinois"), and [Will](/wiki/Will_County,_Illinois "Will County, Illinois") counties in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), and is a south-west suburb of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). The population was 17,629 as of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The village is situated on a hillside along the south banks of the [Des Plaines River](/wiki/Des_Plaines_River "Des Plaines River"). It overlooks [Waterfall Glen's](/wiki/Waterfall_Glen_Forest_Preserve "Waterfall Glen Forest Preserve") [Midwestern Bluff Savanna](/wiki/Oak_savanna "Oak savanna") on the opposite side. Lemont is home to [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory") and other heavy industrial sites, and has a substantial European immigrant population.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lemont,_Illinois&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Before European settlers arrived in Lemont, Native Americans traveled the [Des Plaines River](/wiki/Des_Plaines_River "Des Plaines River") in birch bark canoes on trading trips between the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan"). The native [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") lived off the land in this area, directly using natural resources for food, shelter, clothing and medicine. In the 18th century, French [voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs") traveled down the Des Plaines River, trading Native Americans metal, beads and cloth for animal furs.

Lemont was originally known as *Keepataw* (after a [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi") chief) and a post office was established in 1840 as *Keepatau*. After that, it was named *Athens* and then *Palmyra*. The name *Lemont* (literally, 'the mountain' in French) was chosen in 1850 at the suggestion of Lemuel Brown, the postmaster and justice of the peace, or perhaps by his brother Nathaniel Brown.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Established in 1836, the village of Lemont stands as one of the oldest American communities in northeastern Illinois. It is historically significant for its role in transforming the northern region of the state from a sparsely settled frontier to a commercial, agricultural, and industrial region that supplied Chicago and areas beyond with commodities. Lemont is also unique in boasting an authentic historic district that remains intact and has been continually used since the 19th century. In 2016, the Lemont Downtown Historic District was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Both Lemont's history and architectural uniqueness connect to the [Illinois and Michigan Canal](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal") (I&M Canal). Construction of the I&M Canal began in 1837 and stands as one of the last major canal undertakings in the United States (the [Hennepin Canal](/wiki/Hennepin_Canal_Parkway_State_Park "Hennepin Canal Parkway State Park") opened in 1907). When it was completed in 1848, it provided a continuous waterway stretching from [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") (through the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron") and Lake Michigan to Chicago, then through the I&M Canal for 97 miles (156 km) entering the [Illinois River](/wiki/Illinois_River "Illinois River") at [LaSalle, Illinois](/wiki/LaSalle,_Illinois "LaSalle, Illinois"), to the Mississippi River, to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans")) to the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico").

Immigrant workers, mostly [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"), settled in Lemont to work on the canal and later moved along the corridor of the canal, improving farms within the many communities that sprang up along it.

In digging, workers discovered Lemont yellow [dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(rock) "Dolomite (rock)"), a harder and finer grained version of [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"). This delayed digging of the canal, but was the start of the area's second industry, [quarrying](/wiki/Quarrying "Quarrying"). By the mid-19th century, limestone quarrying took over as the main economic factor in Lemont and sustained its growth. The town's important major buildings were faced with the Lemont limestone, abundant in local quarries. Today, 38 of those buildings remain as the Lemont downtown district. Lemont limestone was used to build the [Chicago Water Tower](/wiki/Chicago_Water_Tower "Chicago Water Tower"), a building that "gained special significance as one of the few buildings to survive the destructive path of the [Great Chicago Fire](/wiki/Great_Chicago_Fire "Great Chicago Fire") of 1871".[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the early years, this stone was known as "[Athens Marble](/wiki/Sugar_Run_Dolomite "Sugar Run Dolomite")" as a nod to its place of origin.[[7]](#cite_note-7) An 1859 item in the *Chicago Daily Tribune* had this to say: “The Athens and DesPlaines quarries, situated on the Illinois and Michigan canal, embrace 335 acres of the finest stone in the West, known as “Athens Marble”. This stone has a high reputation for color, durability and beauty, which renders it quite an article of commerce”.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Cargo and passengers were transported on the I&M until the early 20th century, when the wider, deeper [Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal](/wiki/Chicago_Sanitary_and_Ship_Canal "Chicago Sanitary and Ship Canal") was built parallel to it. The Sanitary Canal is still used today as part of the [Illinois Waterway](/wiki/Illinois_Waterway "Illinois Waterway") system.

Lemont's motto is "Village of Faith", and its church spires reflect the many ethnic groups who came here to quarry stone, dig the Sanitary and Ship Canal and work in other industries.

Lemont is credited with being the largest recruiting station for the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and the Old Stone Church, built in 1861 of limestone, was used as a recruiting depot. It served as the Lemont [Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/Methodist_Episcopal_Church "Methodist Episcopal Church") for 100 years, from 1861 until 1970, when it became home to the Lemont Area Historical Society. The oldest building in Lemont, it now serves as a museum and is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").

During the Civil War, Lemont was required to sign up 33 soldiers, the village recruited 293 soldiers; only 63 returned. The Lemont Civil War Memorial Committee was formed to build a memorial to honor Lemont's Civil War veterans. The monument was dedicated in 2008 in Legion Park at the east end of Main Street, opposite the Metra Station. Of the 293 soldiers sent to fight in the war, only 243 names of the enlisted soldiers are known. Among them is Cpl. John Warden, the only Lemont resident ever awarded the Medal of Honor.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

By 1854, railroads transported goods faster than water, and the I&M became obsolete as Lemont evolved into a railroad community; the village was incorporated on June 9, 1873.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Increasingly, the canal was used to carry wastes away from Chicago. In 1900, the larger Sanitary and Ship Canal went into operation, carrying both wastes and larger, more modern [barges](/wiki/Barge "Barge"). All use of the I&M Canal ended in 1933, with the opening of the canal's modern successor—the Illinois Waterway.

By about 1920, the quarries declined as styles changed and builders began to use [Bedford limestone](/wiki/Indiana_Limestone "Indiana Limestone") from [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and less expensive materials like concrete.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [Metallurgical Laboratory](/wiki/Metallurgical_Laboratory "Metallurgical Laboratory") of the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") moved into the nearby [Argonne Forest preserves](/wiki/Palos_Forest_Preserves "Palos Forest Preserves") to carry out [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi")'s work on nuclear reactors for the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"). After the war, [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory") was designated as the first national laboratory in the United States on July 1, 1946.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1984, President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") signed legislation establishing the Illinois & Michigan Canal National Heritage Corridor as the nation's first [National Heritage Corridor](/wiki/National_Heritage_Corridor "National Heritage Corridor"). The status recognizes the historic importance of this region and the waterway that connected Lake Michigan and the Illinois River. Today, it is a 100-mile-long (160 km) cultural park between Chicago and LaSalle/[Peru](/wiki/Peru,_Illinois "Peru, Illinois"), representing an ongoing partnership between the public and private sectors created to achieve a successful mixture of preservation, public use and industrial activity.

[Sacred architecture](/wiki/Sacred_architecture "Sacred architecture") is a strong suit of Lemont, whose skyline is dominated by two landmark religious edifices: the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") Temple of Greater Chicago and [SS. Cyril and Methodius](/wiki/SS._Cyril_and_Methodius_in_Lemont "SS. Cyril and Methodius in Lemont") church in the [Polish Cathedral style](/wiki/Polish_Cathedral_style "Polish Cathedral style"). Both are situated on the sides of hills, giving an even more dramatic backdrop to their monumental architecture.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Tornadoes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lemont,_Illinois&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Tornadoes")]

On June 13, 1976, at 5:18 PM, a killer [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") struck Lemont and took three lives. 23 were injured, 87 homes were destroyed and 82 more were damaged. Damage to the high school alone was estimated at $500,000 (equivalent to $2,830,000 in 2025[[12]](#cite_note-inflation-US-12)). Many people reported watching neighbors' homes explode, implode, shattering before their eyes. Cited as an unusual tornado, it backed up on its path before heading North, somewhat parallel to its path of origin.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) See [Tornadoes of 1976](/wiki/Tornadoes_of_1976#June_13 "Tornadoes of 1976") for more information on the outbreak.

On March 27, 1991, Lemont was again hit by a tornado.[[16]](#cite_note-16) See [Tornadoes of 1991](/wiki/Tornadoes_of_1991#March_26–29 "Tornadoes of 1991") for more information on the outbreak.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lemont,_Illinois&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the 2021 census gazetteer files, Lemont has a total area of 8.74 square miles (22.64 km2), of which 8.37 square miles (21.68 km2) (or 95.71%) is land and 0.38 square miles (0.98 km2) (or 4.29%) is water.[[17]](#cite_note-gaz2021-17)

The village has 10 exclaves.[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census")[[21]](#cite_note-21) there were 17,629 people, 5,911 households, and 4,606 families residing in the village. The population density was 2,017.05 inhabitants per square mile (778.79/km2). There were 6,773 housing units at an average density of 774.94 per square mile (299.21/km2). The racial makeup of the village was 89.29% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.91% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 0.11% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 2.45% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.03% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 1.38% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 5.83% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") people of any race were 7.12% of the population.

There were 5,911 households, out of which 38.9% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 67.64% were married couples living together, 7.58% had a female householder with no husband present, and 22.08% were non-families. 18.74% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.06% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 3.27 and the average family size was 2.85.

The village's age distribution consisted of 25.1% under the age of 18, 6.5% from 18 to 24, 20.5% from 25 to 44, 29.6% from 45 to 64, and 18.3% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 43.5 years. For every 100 females, there were 100.8 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 94.7 males.

The median income for a household in the village was $114,509, and the median income for a family was $133,456. Males had a median income of $75,542 versus $43,283 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the village was $45,675. About 4.4% of families and 5.1% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 5.9% of those under age 18 and 5.3% of those age 65 or over.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lemont,_Illinois&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Sports")]

In 2006, the Lemont Little League All-Star team traveled to the 60th [Little League World Series](/wiki/Little_League_World_Series "Little League World Series") in [South Williamsport, Pennsylvania](/wiki/South_Williamsport,_Pennsylvania "South Williamsport, Pennsylvania"). The team, which represented the Great Lakes region, received 4th place after losing to the [Beaverton, Oregon](/wiki/Beaverton,_Oregon "Beaverton, Oregon"), team and was the only team to beat the champions from [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)"). Upon returning home, the Lemont team received a parade down State Street in honor of their achievements.

The [BMW Championship (PGA Tour)](/wiki/BMW_Championship_(PGA_Tour) "BMW Championship (PGA Tour)"), and the prior [Western Open](/wiki/Western_Open "Western Open"), were held in unincorporated Lemont township between 1991 and 2007, and 2009 to 2011. Cog Hill Golf & Country Club has subsequently rejected incorporation into Lemont and has agreed to incorporate into Palos Park. The tournament was played at [Cog Hill Golf & Country Club](/wiki/Cog_Hill_Golf_%26_Country_Club "Cog Hill Golf & Country Club"), which was founded in 1927. The [Ruffled Feathers Golf Club](/wiki/Ruffled_Feathers_Golf_Club "Ruffled Feathers Golf Club") is located in Lemont.

In 2008, the girls' 11-year-old Lemont Little League All-Star team traveled to [Beardstown, Illinois](/wiki/Beardstown,_Illinois "Beardstown, Illinois"), for the state tournament and won the state title.

In 2009, a new group of 11-year-old All-Stars from Lemont won the State Championship for a second consecutive year.

The Lemont High School varsity cheerleading team placed first at the IHSA State Championships in the years 2009, 2010, 2011, 2014, and 2017.

In 2020, the Lemont High School wrestling program won the 2020 IHSA Class 2A Dual Team State Championship and set a school record for wins.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lemont,_Illinois&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lemont,_Illinois&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Lemont has a [station](/wiki/Lemont_(Metra) "Lemont (Metra)") on [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra")'s [Heritage Corridor](/wiki/Heritage_Corridor "Heritage Corridor"), which provides weekday rail service between [Joliet](/wiki/Joliet,_Illinois "Joliet, Illinois") and [Chicago Union Station](/wiki/Chicago_Union_Station "Chicago Union Station").

In 2007, the [Interstate 355](/wiki/Interstate_355 "Interstate 355") tollway extension opened, providing Lemont with more direct access to the Chicago expressway system. An interchange is located on 127th Street.
